Query: A 27-year-old woman presents to the office with concerns about her long struggle with her physical appearance since adolescence. She says she has always been "large" and was constantly targeted by her classmates and coworkers for being so. Her main concern at the moment is her acne and unwanted facial hair on her upper lip, for which she often visits a local spa. She has tried numerous diet plans, exercise regimens, and cosmetic products with little to no effect. Recently, she underwent a glucose tolerance test that showed a plasma glucose level of 160 mg/dL (8.9 mmol/L) after 2 hours of a 75 g dose of oral glucose. She has a family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and a menstrual cycle that occurs every 45 days. Her pulse is 72/min and the blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg. On physical examination, her height is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) and her weight is 85 kg (187 lb), and she has severe inflammatory acne over the cheeks and forehead and dark coarse hairs on the back. What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient?

Options:
A: Hypothyroidism
B: Idiopathic hirsutism
C: Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS)
D: Ovarian hyperthecosis

Thyroid disorders may also present with skin, hair, and nail changes, while patients with PCOS or uncontrolled diabetes mellitus may develop acanthosis nigricans. Document [20] (Title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- History and Physical) Polycystic ovarian syndrome and other hyperandrogenic disorders : Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) is the most common cause of hyperandrogenism associated with secondary amenorrhea and is characterized by a combination of menstrual abnormalities, hyperandrogenism, and metabolic dysfunction. [3] [20] These patients will frequently have a longer history of irregular cycles before their current period of amenorrhea, along with evidence of hirsutism (ie, male pattern hair growth), acne, obesity, or insulin resistance. Non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, androgenic tumors, and Cushing syndrome can have similar presentations to PCOS, though androgenic tumors are often associated with much higher levels of abnormal androgen production, potentially leading to virilization (eg, clitoromegaly, deepening of the voice) that is not typically seen in PCOS. Additionally, patients with Cushing syndrome often have other associated symptoms and clinical findings. All patients with secondary amenorrhea should be examined for signs of hyperandrogenism (eg, hirsutism, acne, and virilization) and asked about undesired hair growth that may have already been removed (eg, shaving, waxing, laser).
Document [21] (Title: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education) PCOS is a diagnosis encompassing several symptoms and abnormalities that affect multiple parts of the body. PCOS is primarily a condition of increased male hormone activity, which can manifest through symptoms like male-pattern hair growth (hirsutism), scalp hair loss, and acne, or be detected through hormone blood tests. Additional symptoms may include irregular cycles and infertility. Diagnosis is made by thoroughly assessing symptoms, performing physical exams, conducting laboratory tests, and utilizing imaging techniques. No single test can confirm or exclude the diagnosis of PCOS.

Over 85% of children with NIDDM are either overweight or obese at diagnosis. [23] Acanthosis nigricans is an increased pigmentation and thickness of the skin in intertriginous folds, and it is usually associated with glucose intolerance in children and adolescents. Fasting insulin and glucose should be included in the evaluation of childhood obesity. The risk factors for type 2 non-insulin-dependent diabetes and metabolic syndrome include, children with BMI 85th to 95th percentile along with, immediate family history of type 2 diabetes signs of insulin resistance such as acanthosis nigricans, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and polycystic ovarian syndrome. Children with BMI >95th percentile regardless of family history or associated features. [24]
Document [25] (Title: Hirsutism -- Pearls and Other Issues) The diagnosis of hirsutism requires the careful collection of medical history data, physical examination, appropriate biological, and radiological investigations. Hirsutism can be idiopathic, which is a diagnosis of elimination or secondary to hyperandrogenism that also encompasses other serious health problems, including diabetes, metabolic syndromes, and infertility. In some cases, a tumor process is involved.
Document [26] (Title: Insulin Resistance -- Differential Diagnosis) Lipodystrophy (acquired, localized or generalized): Loss of adipose tissue that results from either genetic or acquired causation and can result in the ectopic deposition of fat in either hepatic or muscular tissue [56] Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) Obesity: Excess body weight is categorized as overweight (BMI of 25 to 29.9), class I obesity (BMI of 30 to 34.9), class II obesity (BMI 35.0 to 39.9), and class III obesity (BMI greater than 40) [57] Hypertension: The most recent ACC/AHA guidelines for the diagnosis of hypertension include systolic pressure greater than or equal to 130 mm Hg or diastolic pressure greater than or equal to 80 mm Hg [58] Hypertriglyceridemia: Elevated triglyceride levels (greater than or equal to 150 mg/dL) Type 1 diabetes Type 2 diabetes Other forms of glucose intolerance (impaired fasting glucose, impaired glucose tolerance, and gestational diabetes)
Document [27] (Title: Hypothyroidism -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination Findings) Most of the patients have normal thyroid examination findings. However, a careful physical examination may reveal some clues since the signs of hypothyroidism are very subtle. Physical examination may be significant for the following: Enlarged thyroid gland Weight gain Slowness of speech and movement Dry skin Coarse and brittle hair Pallor or jaundice Dull facial expressions Macroglossia Bradycardia Pericardial effusion Prolonged ankle reflex relaxation time [2]
Document [28] (Title: Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education) PCOS can run in the family, where not just 1 gene, but the interaction of multiple genes might be responsible for this condition. PCOS is a common medical condition, and it is estimated that in certain ethnicities, it can be present in 1 in 4 women between the ages of 18 and 35 years old. An abnormality in the metabolism and storage of energy, known as insulin resistance, which is also the root cause of diabetes and obesity, has been implicated in causing and perpetuating this abnormal hormonal state. Therefore, the treatments suggested primarily involve at least a 5% weight loss, even in lean PCOS females, with a good response ultimately achieved. These treatments can include metformin and injectable weight loss or diabetes medications. The treatment plan is tailored around the individual health goals of each patient.

She says she has always been "large" and was constantly targeted by her classmates and coworkers for being so. Her main concern at the moment is her acne and unwanted facial hair on her upper lip, for which she often visits a local spa. She has tried numerous diet plans, exercise regimens, and cosmetic products with little to no effect. Recently, she underwent a glucose tolerance test that showed a plasma glucose level of 160 mg/dL (8.9 mmol/L) after 2 hours of a 75 g dose of oral glucose. She has a family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and a menstrual cycle that occurs every 45 days. Her pulse is 72/min and the blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg. On physical examination, her height is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) and her weight is 85 kg (187 lb), and she has severe inflammatory acne over the cheeks and forehead and dark coarse hairs on the back. What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient?

Options:
A: Hypothyroidism
B: Idiopathic hirsutism
C: Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS)
D: Ovarian hyperthecosis

Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721) DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

These entities represent a spectrum of pancreatic endocrine dysfunction, ranging from simple hyperglycemia to specific complications such as neuropathy, as well as hormonal excess states like hyperinsulinemia and glucagon‑secreting tumors. Understanding that all of these conditions are subtypes of diseases of the endocrine pancreas, including diabetes, helps clinicians recognize the varied presentations, anticipate potential complications, and tailor comprehensive patient care.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811) Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157) Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530) Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are clinical manifestations of فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي (primary hypertension). An increase in blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم) is also associated with فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي. While فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي represents essential hypertension, it is conceptually linked to فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (secondary hypertension) as part of the broader hypertension classification, distinguishing primary from secondary causes. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize the hemodynamic signs of primary hypertension and differentiate it from secondary forms for appropriate evaluation and management.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044) Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267) DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053) Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.
